title: 2026 05 04 EW Subcommittee B
displayTitle: 2026-05-03 - Education and Workforce Committee - Employment Leave Bill [Subcommittee B]
description: Employment Leave Bill New Zealand Payroll Practitioners Association (NZPPA) David Jenkins The Farm Office Limited Evelien Baas Leave It To Me Ltd Sandy Price Annick Masselot George McGuinness FaAfuhia Fia Frank Owen-Perry Retail NZ Carolyn Young, Retail NZ Chief Executive Denise Garland, Retail NZ Manager, Advocacy, Advice & Communications Yevgenya Chityan, Retail NZ HR & Business Advisor Hospitality New Zealand Kristy Phillips, Chief Executive Tim Blake, General Counsel Oceana Gold (New Zealand) Limited Alison Paul, SVP Corporate and Legal Affairs
descriptionSummary: Employment Leave Bill. New Zealand Payroll Practitioners Association (NZPPA). David Jenkins

chunks.text: Morning, David. If you can turn your camera on and your mic on. We've got 10 minutes-... with you this morning, including any time for questions. Over to you. All right. Thank you. Thank you for the opportunity to talk to the committee on our submission. So NTVPA, we represent about 2,000 payroll practitioners throughout the country.

I want to cover off a little bit more on each of the kind of four main points that we made in our submission. Our first one was about making payroll providers in New Zealand compliant and accountable for the software that they're providing. At the present time, there are payroll systems that should not be used in the New Zealand environment, and they can get away with this because there's no legislation, there's no certification, there's no IRD or MBIE checks on any software, and from our side, we see issues with this all the time.

So this is one of the things we would like to see built into the act. It was actually in the process of the prior review of the Holidays Act, but in the consultative process, the targeted consultative process that happened October last year, it was written out, and it was clearly stated that it would not be covered, and it's not followed through into the bill, so it's been totally removed from the bill.

And this is a major issue because simplifying payroll software will not solve the problem. That's a big misconception from non-payroll people. Software is written in many different ways, and that's impacting on how businesses use the payroll software, and there's no protection, especially for small business, if they've got, especially overseas payroll software that's non-compliant for the New Zealand environment.

So we're very concerned that it's not in there. So at the present time, an employer can be taken to task by labor inspectorate, an improvement notice, enforceable undertaking, whole range of steps can be taken. We would like to see the same framework applied to payroll providers, so if there's an issue of a payroll system, they're asked to provide evidence to show that they're compliant.

If they're not, an improvement notice can be put on them. If they don't act on that, an enforceable undertaking is put on them. So all the things that you would normally do for an employer, you could actually do for a payroll provider. Now, when MBIE or a labor inspector is talking to an employer, they're talking to one employer. When you're talking about a payroll system, you're talking about a whole industry.

You're talking about hundreds if not thousands of employers that are using that software, and that's why it's important that the software is compliant. So that's why it's not just about one situation, it's talking about software that's used across a whole industry, and this is why it's important. I really like the idea of name and shame.

You've got banning orders for immigration and bad employers. By just threatening name and shame, a lot of these payroll providers that are not compliant would act because they do not want to be put on a list. They do not want to be out there to be shown that they're non-compliant, because it's about selling software, and that's a really good way to quickly get all these payroll systems on track.

But we do need certification, and we do need checks from IR and MBIE on these systems. They are causing major problems. So yes, it's legislation. Yes, it's how it's managed, but it's also the software itself and choices made by payroll providers. There are payroll providers out there that are saying, "Oh, we don't do this calculation. We don't need it."

And then because small businesses are not payroll experts, they see what's on the website that say it's fully compliant with the Holidays Act, and they buy the software thinking it's all been checked by IR, that's another misconception, and then they get hooked in and they've spent all this money, and then they find out the software they're using is incorrect.

So that is a major issue, and that's one part of our submission. Next one is about, we need a payroll specification document for any new act. This is more payroll technical, but we had one when we had the partner labor, when MBIE, the super ministry was formed. They said, "No, not interested.

Go seek independent advice." And that's what's caused a lot of the mess in the Holidays Act, is because we've had no real leadership from MBIE. They duck and dive quite quickly. Any question, it's quite vague. So we need a payroll specification document written for payroll that will support payroll providers to provide compliant software, and that's not what we've got at the moment, and we need it for the new act, and it's got to be for all stakeholders. We do auditing.

We do technical training. We base everything on a payroll spec. All right? So we get you the correct information on how a payroll system runs. That must be part of any new act, and it must be out before the two-year implementation process, so we can actually build a new system, not at the other end. Point three was about remuneration.

That's really good. We fully support any type of process you put into a new act to fix the problems that we've got now, so we can move forward. We don't want to be dragged back in the last six years trying to do recalculations, but there's a real problem that we're seeing right now. So you're saying there's a potential process in the new act that employers will be able to use to fix the problems of the past.

We're seeing employers right now making decisions to say, "Oh, we're not going to do anything now. We know there's problems now. We'll just wait two years or when the act's actually implemented, and then we'll use your new process to basically fix the problems." So that means employees will not be paid, not have their leave fixed until the new act is implemented in years to come, and that is wrongSo it's sending the wrong message.

The labor inspectorate needs to send a clear message to all employers, if they've got a problem now, it must be fixed now, and if it's found that they didn't act on it when the new act's in place and they're asking for this new remuneration process to be used, that will not be accommodated. And that means potentially a whole range of extra legislative requirements are put onto that employer, because that is basically wrong, where an employee should have been paid correctly right now.

Thank you. There's a big section of our submission, and we honestly ran out of time, about issues, and we just had to put it in bullet form. I would probably need another month to write up and put the detail behind it. This act is, or the bill is actually kind of replacing the overly complex act we've got now with another set of rules, calculations, and tests, which will create another set of complexity. And a lot of it, it's written for software, but it's not written for end-to-end payroll processing. That's operational payroll, and there's going to be a lot of

stops and starts when you're trying to process pay. If I process pay on a Monday, I've got to get that money in the bank by the end of the day. Some of the ways, this, how the act works, it's sort of like saying, "I've got to stop. I've got to go and ask a few people. I've got to do a few things, got to do a few tests." That doesn't work in operational payroll. So it hasn't really been thought out in that regard.

So there are problems, and I see it's going to step or create a new set of problems for payroll going forward. I say devil in the detail. There's a lot of detail missing from this bill that payroll would need to feel if it's workable. And there's a lot missing. And I'm kind of scared that over the two-year implementation process, that's when the detail will come. Well, that's when we're building systems, and we can't keep rebuilding the system from scratch as new things come up. So a lot of the detail needs to be up front when

the act is actually passed, and that's one of the issues that we have in regards to how this act's going to be implemented over a two-year period. I've kind of pushed it through because I don't know about the 10 minutes for any questions. So over to Evan. Thanks so much, David. Is there any questions, Phil or Grant?

No, that was extremely interesting and different from so many of the submissions we've had, so very helpful. Thanks, David. Yeah. Thanks, Grant. So David, just on the point around a document that comes with the specifications, that wouldn't be defined though in the act.

Your expectation is that just happens as part of the implementation of it, or are you expecting that there's a reference to the requirement for that specifications list in the act? So at the present time, IR, sorry, I've just come up from the flu. IR has a specification document that they release for the 1st of April changes.

Yeah. And that's not part of legislation, but all the legislation feeds into that document. We need some sort of guarantee, some sort of ongoing support from MB that, one, the document's going to be created-... and then it's going to have ongoing support as case law, as other things change, it's updated.

So that means you've got a base document that all payroll providers, all auditors, anyone can actually go to say, "This is how the payroll system needs to be built." So it's an implementation commitment, not a legislative reference, but take the point. Thank you. Hey, look, really good submission. Thank you for taking the time to join us this morning.
Members, we are now going to move to our second submission from The Farm Office Limited.

Good morning. We've got 10 minutes with you this morning, including any time for submissions, so I'll hand straight over to you. Hello. So my name is Evelyn Bass. I'm actually a member of the NZPPA. So I'm a payroll provider, an outsourced payroll provider. I do payroll for farms, gyms, cafes, and liquor stores, and I currently have major problems implementing the Holiday Act because of the flexible and varied working arrangements, and this, the new Employee Leave Act is basically,

idea is to replace the Holiday Act, makes it less complicated, makes it fair simple, and the principle of accruing leave for every hour worked is great. And then all the limitations and accepting exceptions surrounding this by basically linking it to agreed hours and rosters and notional rosters, then create, in my view, unnecessary complexity for flexible work.

And flexible work is increasing, not decreasing, and creates unfairness to employees because they force employees to create limitations in employment agreements that would then give employees less leave than they think they should have. Because if you accrue leave based solely on hours worked, it's clear, it's easy, it's fair for both the employers and the employees. It will give an employee time off based on the time they actually work, and they get more leave as they work more.

So if somebody is contracted to work 40 hours a week but always works 50-They will actually get 50 hours worth of leave for every week of leave that they take, which means they get the pay they expect, and they get the leave they expect. But with these limitations where your agreed hours are 40, but you work 50, you create a complexity in the payroll that makes the payroll very difficult for anyone who's got employees that really work in a flexible way.

Because on a farm, one week they work seven days a week, the next week they only work four days a week, and their rosters go over three or four weeks. They vary it during the season. And there's a lot of people on farms that actually work as and when they can. So these are people that are hard to employ because they've got other commitments.

So they work if and when they can, but they are permanent employees. But if I try to apply the rules in the current act to those employees, it would be impossible. The only way I can see to apply these rules to employees like that is that the employer and the employee would agree every Sunday before payday as to what their agreed hours are, and then communicate that to payroll, and payroll will then apply that to payroll they process. Otherwise, I cannot see any way to apply the rules.

Because the roster changes, because yes, of course, there is a roster, and then people have day off. They have employment leave, bereavement leave, sick leave, whatever, and the roster changes for everybody in the business. And so it basically makes it impossible to say these are agreed hours because we can't put agreed hours in the employment agreement.

You have a roster, but the roster changes. So how are you going to say these are additional hours, or these are not additional hours? I simply can't see a way to make it work, unless the employers incredibly severely change the way they work.

And the way it would work would be unfair to the employees because what the employer would do is put agreed hours in employment agreement, which are the least hours an employee might be expected to work in a week. So you might say you work 15 hours a week, but there might be weeks where they work 50, but they would only get leave based on the 15 hours.

And I don't think employees would be happy if they only got 15 hours of leave. They have got the compensation of the leave payment for the other hours, but it doesn't give them leave, and it doesn't give them a fair payment when they are on leave. So that's my main point, that the principle is great, but the limitations to link it back to agreed hours creates a huge problem for any truly flexible work.

Okay. Thank you. Mm. Any questions? So Evelyn, is your view that it would create an incentive for employers to scale down the number of agreed committed hours, and that would have the effect of casualizing more employment? Well, basically, these are people that have a permanent job- Mm... with flexible hours.

So there are no agreed hours in the current employment agreement at all. Mm. But if you force people to create agreed hours, then what they would do is put the lowest amount of hours that people work- Yep... as agreed hours, because you don't want to pay people for hours they don't work.

Mm-hmm. But that's not fair to the employee. Mm. But you don't give the employer much choice than doing that. Mm. And I can give you another example. I do payroll for a dairy farm where the son works on the dairy farm, and the rest of the dairy farm is staffed by students.

They're close to Lincoln University. So everybody gets paid per hour. They get the living wage. Mm. But their roster changes based on when people can work every single week. Mm. Nobody's got agreed hours in their agreement. And basically, once this act comes in, in the way it is now, those people will either have to sit down with every employee every week to then agree on the hours they already worked to then be able to do the payroll.

Mm. Or these people would completely have to change the way they run their farm and fire their student employees. Mm. I don't think that's the intended way the law is supposed to work. Mm. So I just tried to apply the proposed law to all these situations, and I can't.

Yeah. It's not possible. Yeah. You can't do it. It's not workable. Mm. And to me, it's kind of going against the whole spirit of the law and the change of the law because- Mm... work is becoming more flexible. People do want more flexibility in their life. They do want to work from home. They do want to work when they want to work.

And yet, by building this relation with agreed hours into the law, you basically recreate the problems that there were with the Holidays Act. Yeah. Where anyone who doesn't work regular hours-gets punished in a way that you can't find. In the Holidays Act, you couldn't define a week and you couldn't define a day.

Mm. In the current law, you can't define agreed hours and additional hours. I mean, different words, but in essence, the same problem. Thank you. That's really helpful. Thank you, Evelyn. In your submission, you make the point that you don't think it's appropriate that leave is accrued while on parental leave.

Just wanting to confirm that you understand that this bill removes that provision. Am I understanding your comment correctly? No. The new bill basically pays people for accruing leave, paid leave, while they're on parental leave. But to me, that is a minor point, because I don't deal with that very often. But the point I'm making now, I deal with it every single week, and it's going to make my work impossible in to the sense that if the law passes as it is right now, I may seriously reconsider my career choice by the time it comes in.

Okay. Grant, quick final question. Yeah, just very quick. So, I take your point, what you're saying. But just in terms of the casualization and so forth, I'd imagine students, most of them, would be quite happy just to receive the money, extra money in terms of their holiday pay, as opposed to necessarily worrying about accrued leave because of the nature of what students they are in that casualization.

So all these people actually choose to accrue leave instead of having it paid out. So, no. Okay. That's interesting. I mean, you might think so, but the answer to that currently is no. Yes, I understand. Thank you. Okay. Appreciate that. Thanks very much, Evelyn. That's all we have time for. Appreciate you taking the time to submit to us this morning.
We're now moving to the submission from Leave It To Me Limited. So if we can invite Sandy to the... You've frozen.

Can you hear me? Yeah. Just froze for a moment. I might be now unmuted. Hello. If you could turn your video on, please, so we can see you, that'd be appreciated. Yes, I'm just trying to look for where this is. There we go. Great. We've got 10 minutes with you this morning, including any time for questions.

Yes. Thank you. So if you could introduce- Okay. My name's Sandy Price. I'm a business consultant and I've got Anne with me. She does a lot of our payroll. So I run a retail business and we also have worked with quite a wide range of clients for payroll, as well as a whole lot of other things.

We share a lot of concerns with your previous panelist, so I won't repeat them because I think she's made some incredibly valuable points. The main thing is the issue of a lot of people who start out on casual, and particularly in hospitality, eventually morph into permanent staff who should be allowed to accrue leave because the issue is one of how you actually take your leave, that you actually do get holidays, that is the entire point, as well as being paid fairly for those holidays.

So people who've gone from being casual with 8% paid then going on to accruing leave, at the moment, they lose out because of the changes at the year-end. I think the idea of accruing based on the hours and financial value is definitely worthwhile, and it almost leads to a separate employment category, because there are a lot of people who do have flexible work for the reasons outlined by your previous commentator.

Those people might have medical issues. They've got children. They've got other commitments. They've got multiple jobs. And they're happy, excuse me, to work in that industry. But that industry does come with a huge amount of variability in hours available through seasons and through things like events. So, being able to actually take three out of the four weeks of paid leave requires us to be able to calculate what actually is an appropriate time period, often taken in the low season when the hours are less, but they will have accrued holiday pay at a

higher rate. So I think it's important for any changes to reflect the fact that you're accruing leave based on time as well as value. But when you take your leave, the intention is for people to have effectively three weeks of paid holiday, plus they can take another week of paid. And a lot of people that we deal with would prefer to be paid.

When they change that employment type, it actually requires them to take a pay cut. You're going from 8% pay as you go to accruing leave, and then they lose 8%. So being able to accrue it at a point where they will receive that 8% guaranteed at a later date is really important. The other thing that doesn't seem to have come up, I haven't read all of the details, but a lot of people are paid in a variety of ways.

You've also got allowances like shift allowances. You've got overtime. You've got steps that are worked at time and a half. You've got commission that's payable. Sometimes this is on top of a minimum wage arrangement, and sometimes it's in lieu of. You've also got people who get paid bonuses, and it's not always clear in the employment contract if those bonuses should be attracting holiday pay. And then you've got others on a piece rate.

So your accrual of paid value has a whole lot of factors to it. Your accrual of time is important in terms of taking that leave, so it needs to be taken as a week, or an equivalent to a week, because the whole point is that everybody gets four weeks of leave and supposedly two weeks of sick leave.

So how do you make sure that they actually get their entitlement in time as well as value? Because you're accruing it on a different basis if you have a variable workload. So I think perhaps one solutionWould be a hybrid employment type because it is a growing thing. It doesn't mean that employers are taking advantage of employees, because it is usually of mutual benefit to have a negotiated roster. So if you're able to accrue leave on the basis of 8%, as long as you can take your leave, particularly those who work nearly full-time.

If you're part-time, you're quite happy often to receive the money instead of the time. But if you're a full-time person, you still need to be able to take that time for rest and recuperation. So it's really important to be able to work out what that equivalency is. So that's my main point about the holidays. The second one is about sick leave. I do believe it was an oversight in the previous act to allow for 10 days of sick leave. There are a lot of people who have extremely variable patterns, a lot of part-timers.

You have the situation where someone who works one 10-hour day per week, for example, who can get 10 weeks of sick leave, and that to me is a bit of a nonsense. It then makes it an incentive for an employer to have that person working five 2-hour days in order for them to get 2 weeks of sick leave, and that creates a whole lot of other issues. So I think the importance with the sick leave is it does need to be proportional, and it does need to come back to a two-week equivalent, not in a set number of days. So there are definitely some challenges.

The software companies have risen to the occasion in many cases, but most of them don't allow for accurate recording of the actual days worked or of the roster at the point that it was set. And that would probably need to change. We also find that we have to enter into a lot of discussions with employers about the otherwise working day. For example, someone with a casual work pattern, the only time you really know that they're sick is if they were scheduled to work and didn't. And the other part is with stat days, if they

were not rostered to work a stat day. But had that been a normal week, it's really obvious from the pattern that that would have been a stat day and they are eligible. So that leads to a lot of discussions about the otherwise working day, and I think that is something that's important to keep, because there is currently a method of calculation and a recommended system that will pass if you were audited by the labor department or whatever the current equivalent is, if you were getting it wrong. So it is important that employers and

payroll people and employees do have a system that they can use to calculate those things. Just one last thing is about the accrual of parental leave. I don't see how you can accrue time and value if you're not actually at work. You may accrue time off, which might be important if you're a new parent, but if you haven't worked for six months, for example, then you're not necessarily accruing any actual paid leave. So how would that work? I think it is a bit of a nonsense. But you do need to be mindful of the time as well as the

value. The other thing which I'm particularly concerned about is the idea that casual staff should get 12.5% of their rate of pay from the day that they are employed. That's on the basis that they would receive their sick pay bundled in with 8% holidays. That's assuming that they're sick.

Most people don't take any of their sick leave, and yet this proposal is to pay them for it in advance, and that is actually completely inappropriate. I think people can accrue leave, but new employees who are a new factor for a lot of employers, there's no trust, there's no knowledge in having people able to take all types of leave from day one, I think is a disincentive for employers to take on people who potentially might be a risk to them. So I think it will lead to some unintended consequences in terms of employer behavior. Thank you. I think that's all I've got.

Thanks very much. We've got time for a couple of quick questions, members. Anyone got a question? No. Sandy, thank you so much for your presentation this morning and submission. I really appreciate it. We are going to move on to our next submitter now. Thank you. Where we're going to invite, Annick to the virtual seat.

Annick, if you'd like to- Yes... kick around- I'm trying to find a video in here. Oh, no worry. We'll give you a moment. Sorry. Here we are. Oh. Is that on? Yes, it looks like it's coming on. There we go. Oh, excellent. Thank you. Good morning. We've got five minutes with you this morning.

So, that includes any time for questions. Over to you. Thank you. Thank you for giving me the opportunity to speak. My name is Annick Masselo, and I'm a professor of law at the University of Canterbury. I have extensive experience in employment law, and in the area of gender equality both in New Zealand and in Europe.

I wish to raise concerns. So in contrast to the previous speakers, I wish to raise concern on the idea of having a pro rata calculation of sick leave under the Employment Leave Bill. It's likely to disproportionately impact women, and by extension, children and other dependents.

Evidence from New Zealand Ministry for Women shows that women in Aotearoa, New Zealand, continue to take care of most of the family's responsibility, and women are more likely to have primary care for the unpaid care, and family responsibility. So, they do take care of children, elderly, people with disabilityAnd this responsibility directly affect their ability to participate fully in the education training and the labor market.

This is reflected by employment pattern. Data from Statistic New Zealand indicates that about 30% of women work part-time compared to about just 12% of men. But what we see is that those are not choices that are made individually, but they're shaped by structural factors, which includes high cost of childcare in New Zealand, which is one of the most unaffordable in the OECD country.

In this context, it is important to note that New Zealand does not have a specific entitlement to care leave, and that's really in contrast to many other countries. Sick leave serves a double purpose. It serves the purpose of taking care of the person who is sick, the individual who is sick, but also to the employee, but also for the employee to look after dependents.

And because women are more likely to carry the caregiving, they're also more likely to need more of the leave. But because of the pro rata, they will be entitled to less leave, but will still require more leave. So the pro rata system compounds inequalities.

Women, it creates structural disadvantage. Those with the greater care responsibility will receive the least access to leave. In effect, the bill risk penalizing care work, which we know is essential for families, communities, and the economy at large. And without a separate entitlement for care leave, the burden falls disproportionately on women.

In the current New Zealand context, where pay equity has faced recent setbacks and child poverty is a pressing issue, it's critical that the legislation does not further entrench inequalities. And this bill, as it is drafted at the moment, does exactly that. So I respectfully put forward that I think the pro rata approach to sick leave should be rethought.

And I know people really like this idea of equality, equality, but it's very formal equality, and also that the committee should consider the idea of introducing a dedicated sick leave to ensure more equitable outcome. Thank you for your time, and I welcome any questions. Thank you very much. Members, we've got time for a couple of quick questions if anyone likes to ask a question.

I don't have a question, Annick, but that's a really helpful perspective. Thank you. And not one we've had from other submitters yet, so I really value that. Thank you very much for taking time to submit to us. We'll move on to our next submission. I'd like to invite George McGuinness to the virtual table, please.

Hi, everyone. Thank you for- Morning, George. We've got five minutes with you this morning, including any time for questions. Over to you. Cool. Sweet. Hi, thank you for giving me the opportunity of having an oral submission this morning. I'm a year 13 student in Auckland, and based on my experiences with my own family, I researched and wrote a report for school on the relationship between physically active work and health outcomes in New Zealand. From that research background and my interest in law and policy, that's why I decided to submit for this bill.

While I appreciate the work that this bill does, I have two main concerns for workers in physically active jobs. My first and main concern is the blanket accrual rate. The bill applies at 0.0385 hours of sick leave per standard hour worked uniformly across every industry. This seems fair, but the idea that a construction worker doing grueling physical work rain or shine, having the same minimum sick leave entitlement as a worker sitting at a cushy desk is kind of disconnected from reality.

And the evidence supports this. A 2021 peer-reviewed study published following over 3,500 workers found that those in the highest physical workload quartile took 2.1 times more sick leave days than those in the lowest physical workload quartile. And that's even after adjusting for age, gender, and lifestyle.

I posit that the bill should be amended to have a tiered sick leave accrual rate. This can be administered proportionate to preexisting ACC levy rate groups to prevent any more hoops from being introduced as this bill aims. And despite the 2.1 times evidence, I propose that it should be a very modest 50% rise as an evidence-based starting point for officials to refine.

One limitation that I acknowledge with this suggestion is that the classifications are by industry rather than individual role. But this creates smaller inconsistencies in the current framework that ignores physically demanding work entirely. My second concern is the LCP framework. We've already heard from lots of the earlier panelists today about how the LCP framework creates issues with workers getting sick leave, so I won't repeat that. But I think it's worth taking their analysis to emphasize the severe impact on workers in physically demanding jobs, who are often the ones

who go beyond their contracted hours because their work is dictated by the job. For example, a builder staying longer because the roof needs to be built before the rain comes in, or a farmer working between their hours that they can get their crop sowed. So for workers in these physically demanding roles, the LCP mechanism creates a dangerous incentive that is detrimental not only to the workers, but ultimately costs employers more than it saves them.Because a worker who is continually incentivized to push through exhaustion and illness to collect LCP, operating like

dangerous machinery or carrying heavy loads, is an ACC claim waiting to happen. This worker on ACC for months or even years loses significant productivity, cost replacement, hiring and training, and a WorkSafe investigation. I feel like this vastly exceeds the cost that the worker taking more piecemeal sick leave throughout the year.

Therefore, for workers in WorkSafe classified priority industries or those on the highest ACC employer levies, I think that the LCP payment should be removed entirely and replaced with leave accrual on all hours worked. This change also means that industries like healthcare, public sector, hospitality, retail, and tourism, who largely drove the Holidays Act reform, get to keep all the bill's LCP framework, as it doesn't have the same risks to their worker safety. But finally, even if you don't think anything I say stands today, at the very least, I believe that an

independent occupational health impact assessment should be commissioned within two years of commencement, with disaggregated analysis of impacts on Māori and Pasifika workers, given their over-representation in physically active worker groups, and thus their health is exceptionally vulnerable in this bill.

I think that ultimately today, the bill's simplification of employment law is great, but over-simplifying risks missing the nuance of how employment is fundamentally different for people in physically active versus sedentary roles. Thank you. I'm happy to answer any questions, but I know we're quite short on time today. Thanks very much, and really impressive to see the submission. I've got a question just around the point you make around the sick leave, particularly the challenge that can create for Māori and Pasifika. Given that the LCP rate essentially

assumes they're going to take all of their sick leave, so it pays them out all of their sick leave, and that the casual work is probably something that is more widely used in some of the roles that you're talking about, wouldn't this therefore be a benefit to those groups because they're actually getting all of that sick leave pay right up front?

I think that ultimately it's better that workers receive their sick leave entitlement rather than receiving LCP frameworks from hours they work over. And this is because, for example, in these physically demanding work roles, like for example, a construction worker, when they're working past their contracted hours, for example, it's not like a desk worker like yourself, having to sit at a desk for extra two hours.

They're laboring, carrying, causing strain on themself, causing strain on their cardiovascular system. And so ultimately, while they're accruing these extra LCP payments, ultimately they're also requiring more rest and recovery after doing all that extra work. And so as soon as they go past their allocated sick leave entitlement that they accrue from the hours that they contracted, ultimately they can't afford to have unpaid leave. And so they will end up

coming to work physically tired and physically drained. And I feel like ultimately that is more of a risk, this incentive to work extra hours instead of taking rest and taking time to recover is a dangerous incentive to have for workers. Got it. Very quick, we are over time, but very quick final question from Grant McCallum.

Yeah. As a farmer who earns a living doing physical job, unlike possibly some of my colleagues I know. Yeah. Thanks, George. Really impressed. I really enjoyed your submission. But I just think, so the stats actually show that people doing physical work have a greater use of sick leave.

Is that what your research showed you? Yeah. From the peer-reviewed study I got from the International Archives of Occupation Environmental Health- Mm... it showed that workers in physically demanding roles take 2.1 times more sick leave than those in less demanding roles. Well, I can assure you I certainly didn't, but anyway, thanks.

I appreciate that, mate. Cheers. Bye. All right. Thanks very much, George. Appreciate you taking the time to join us today. Really impressive to see you. Members, we're moving on to Frank Owen Pirie for a five-minute submission. Thanks, Frank, for joining us this morning, including any time for questions. Over to you.
Just give Frank a chance to unmute and turn on his video.
Can't hear you yet, Frank.
Great. There we go. Frank, we can see you, and I think we can hear you. Do speak for us. No, we can't hear you. We can see you, though.

If you're struggling, we can see if we can move to our next submitter, and come back to you at the end if you're able to solve itOkay. So, is there any chance that Retail New Zealand, Carolyn, that you're online? I'm sure you're here and prepped and ready, so hopefully I can call on you early.

Oh, look at that. Fantastic. It's the thing about retail, you've got to be there when the customer's ready to come in the door, eh? Absolutely. Look, thank you very much for bringing us in. And just for those of you I haven't met, I'm Carolyn Young, Chief Executive of Retail NZ, and my colleague- I'm Denise, Advocacy Manager at Retail NZ... and my other colleague, Eve Cameron.

Business and HR Advisor at Retail NZ. Great. So look, we take our submission this week, we just wanted to highlight a few key points that really are critical from a retail perspective. So look, Retail NZ members support the introduction of a leave compensation payment framework for additional hours worked by non-casual employees, and this provides a much simpler and more transparent alternative to current navigating rules under the Holidays Act.

There are concerns about the LCP rate, though, of 12.5% being too high. We understand that the intention of the LCP is to take into account the absence of both annual and sick leave for casual workers. However, permanent employees would be entitled to receive the same LCP rate for additional hours worked, and our members have questioned how that calculation has been put in place at 12.5% when they already have a sick leave entitlement and annual leave entitlements.

So we recognize that the LCP is intended to simplify leave payments and the calculations under the Holidays Act, but the biggest impact of the new LCP payment will be for those businesses that are small regional or have large part-time workforces. In retail environments, their staff regularly work additional hours, where reliance on flexibility is often necessary to manage seasonal demand, business continuity, and unplanned absences from some employers, will incur additional wage costs regardless of thorough workforce planning

practices. As you said at the start, when you're in retail, you've got to open the store, and so you've got to have people that are available, and if people call in sick, you have to often call in casual people or part-timers and at short notice. So it's an environment that employs significant number of part-time workers.

We're open seven days a week, often businesses are open late as well, so 9:00 to 5:00 doesn't cut it in retail. So, many of these workers value part-time work and take on additional hours that are mutually agreed. So, flexibility we believe is important.

Increasing the rate from 8% to 12.5% represents a material and immediate labor cost increase, particularly for retailers. We support the introduction of a clearer based approach to determining otherwise working days for employees where variable schedules occur regularly.

Members have welcomed greater certainty compared to the subjective and often contested tests under the current act, and we strongly support removing the requirement to pay public holidays that fall after an employee's last day of work. And then, the parental leave is the other one I'll just quickly touch on. Employees returning from parental leave, it's an important way in which we need to transition people back into the workforce. But having access to parental, to having access to annual leave and sick leave at full rates, does put a burden on the

employer, where you've had an employee who hasn't contributed to the business for 12 months, and all of a sudden they've got all of their annual leave that's accumulated over their parental leave is available at their full rate, which is a significant change. And then probably one other small thing I would say would be, just some of our businesses that are franchise businesses and businesses change hands on a semi-regular basis, just clarifying how those changes are implemented when a business changes hands, and thinking a little bit about a

change of ownership and how that impacts anniversary dates for an employment contract. So they'd probably be the things that we'd highlight, and we're really open for questions. Thanks very much, Carolyn. Members, questions for Retail New Zealand? No, very helpful. Thank you.

Just a question around your comment around the 12.5%, and particularly the inclusion of sick leave as part of that calculation. Do you perceive that your members would be more comfortable having that at a lower rate, but then managing sick leave for those casuals outside of that rate into the future? The whole idea was to simplify leave payments, but what I'm hearing is that that might not be what your members would desire.

Yeah, I think really we were looking at, you can correct me if I'm wrong, it was really more about those permanent workers where they work additional hours and how that calculation moves from 8% to 12.5%. We understand in terms of the casual, that how they've worked through the 12.5% where we can live with that, but actually it's the permanent employees where they work additional hours.

Because it often happens in retail where you've got additional shifts or there's different hours, people under mutual agreement will work additional hours, then all of a sudden the rate that they are accumulating, it goes up to 12.5%. That was the key thing, wasn't it? So I suppose the- That was our big concern. Yeah. And it's understood that that additional work doesn't contribute to what would've been the average payout they receive on a sick leave day.

So that additional work would no longer contribute to their sick leave calculation on a sick leave day. Yes, but it would be preferred that the rate for a permanent employee was remained at 8%. Okay. Okay, any other questions? No. Thanks so much. Really appreciate you presenting to our committee again, and I'm sure we'll see you in the not too distant future.

Perfect. Thank you very much. Thank you. Members, we've got one submitter that we're trying to get in touch with, so we will move to... We've got a little bit of a buffer, actually, unless, Clark, we have any of our post-10:00 a.m. submitters ready.

Morning, Mr. Chair. Christie from Hospitality New Zealand is on the call. We'll just need to see whether her general counsel is also available, but we can promote Christie to find out. Okay, let's do that.

Hi, Christie. So are both of you in the same room there? Yes, we are. Good morning. Good morning. Fabulous. You might just want to change that setting so that it doesn't have that blur on the back, because otherwise- Yes. I see... you're going to keep going in and out of focus.

How does that look? Fabulous. Great. So we've got you for 10 minutes this morning, including any time for questions. So I'll hand over to you. Perfect. Well, good morning. Thank you for having us in and allowing us to speak to our submission. Broadly, we support many of the recommendations.

We think there's a bit of a balance in here in terms of gains for both employers and employees, and recognize that that's always a really fine line to be walking. So, thank you for giving us the opportunity to comment through here. Particularly, pro rata of sick leave, that's something that our members have been calling for for a number of years, and in an industry where sometimes people only work two shifts a week, to have them eligible for five weeks of sick leave doesn't always sit well with their more full-time

colleagues. So commend you on that one. We do have some questions around the standard hours, and particularly in terms of employees picking up extra work voluntarily, coming in to work an extra shift if one of their colleagues is unable to, or in terms of very seasonal workplaces where they potentially work more hours in summer and less in winter, and have a balance throughout the year to reach their standard hours.

In my mind, a work pattern makes more sense. We obviously have guaranteed minimum hours within employment contracts. However, depending on seasonality, functions, what's happening within venues, standard hours might actually be higher than that. And then I know there's a lot of questions around casual employees and their entitlement to sick leave. However, from our point of view, there's probably a bigger question of what actually constitutes a casual employee, and what is the nature of that working relationship.

Because if they're a true casual employee, we are of the belief that each time they finish a shift, that is the end of that contract until they are offered and accepted a new shift. So therefore, where does sick leave actually become an entitlement there?

And also would like some clarification around the multiple roles and keeping leave entitlements separate. Again, particularly in regional small businesses, it's not unusual for one of your front of house or kitchen staff potentially might be working part-time and studying. On a school holiday, the cleaner's away for a week and they would like to pick up that cleaning shift, because they have the capability and the time.

What does that then mean for that leave entitlement, and how do we keep that separate? So I think that's sort of the broader points of our submission, and happy to take questions and go into any details. I have my general counsel here who's happy to go into the nitty-gritty for you. Thanks so much. I just want to ask a question about the point you make around that a casual contract ends at the end of each, essentially, engagement. Just wonder, give us a little bit more of a sense of your view on that and how it impacts leave entitlements from your perspective.

Yeah, absolutely. And I guess it's also looking at what's in employment legislation versus employment law. So-... Tim, would you like to speak to it? Yeah, sure. So, the definition of casual employment, and something that you see in a lot of casual employment agreements, is that they say that the employment's not continuous, that the employment relationship ends at the end of each period of engagement, which would typically be a shift, but sometimes it's longer than a shiftBut it's the block of work that the employee has said, "Yes, I will come

in this week and work today," or, "today and tomorrow," or whatever it is. And that's been accepted as the way casual works by the ERA and the Employment Court. And so therefore, automatically you are not within Section 63 (1) of the Holidays Act, which says that employment needs to be continuous for six months as your first limb of when you become entitled to sick leave.

Also, in the other limb of Section 63 of the Holidays Act, of when an employee can become entitled to two sick leave, is when they have worked an average of 10 hours a week over six months, and not less than one hour every week, or not less than 40 hours every month. So you've got to satisfy the first criteria and then one out of two of the second criteria, and if you do meet that test, then the employment is looking pretty regular. You're probably not a true casual.

So in my experience, true casuals are generally not legally entitled to sick leave. And the real confusion, though, is when they are a true casual and when they aren't, because there are a lot of workers on casual employment agreements, but when the ERA looks at the work pattern, they say, "No, no, this is not true casual." And that's a bigger problem for us than confusion around who is a employee and who is a contractor.

And that problem around who's an employee and who's a contractor has been helpfully dealt with by the gateway test in the amendments to the Employment Relations Act. We think a gateway test for saying who is a true casual and who's not a true casual would solve a lot of confusion. Okay. Thank you.

Grant. Yeah. I just want you to pick up on that point you made about if you've got someone who's working for you in the hospitality game, and I'm thinking of particular businesses in the Bay of Islands, my way, who they're really busy in summer, and they've got someone permanently employed for 12 months, and do a lot more hours there and a lot less in the winter period, which is quite normal.

And we had the dairy sector come in and talk about having annualized hours, and sort of averaged out over the year and they've got an arrangement. Is that the kind of sort of thing you're looking for? I'd be interested in this, please. I'm really keen to keep things simple.

Yeah. And recognizing that annualized hours are sometimes not simple to work out if you're a little mom and dad business. Gotcha. But yes, when you're looking at those standard hours and you're going over and above and then having that 12.5% entitlement coming in- Mm-hmm... that doesn't necessarily correlate with what the actual annualized hours would be. So if we can find a simple way to make that happen, or even doing it quarterly- Mm-hmm... depending, or sorry, I should say biannually, depending on the seasonal nature.

But Tim, have you got anything? Yeah. Can I jump in with a comment on that? Because- Yeah... Clause 6 definition of standard hours, I know 6 (2) refers to rostered hours, but the default under 6 (1) seems to be what really is the equivalent of minimum guaranteed hours in the employment agreement. But often minimum guaranteed hours, they're the employer saying, "Well, in a bad week, what's the minimum we can be sure we can offer you?" And for the employee's point of view, "When you're busy with university exams or with kids' school holidays,

what's the minimum number of hours you can commit to us?" So you might have a worker that has a minimum guaranteed hours of, say, 10 a week, but typically works 20 or 25. And so the standard hours aren't really giving you a good ref- the standard hours as defined in the bill aren't really giving you a good reflection of what their typical working week looks like. And I would've thought that you want the annual leave to be accruing based on what a typical week looks like, so that when it comes time for them to take three or four weeks annual leave, they

can get paid based on what a typical week looks like. Yeah. Very helpful. Thank you. Teanau. Yeah. Kia ora. I was listening to your comments around true casual and not true casual. And I was wanting to know how you sort that out now, and how you might foresee the bill helping you or hindering you in your perspective on that aspect.

So I think it would be a way of working out some criteria in the same way that we've done with contractors. And the criteria, I suppose, would start off by saying it was a true casual, saying that there was no obligation to offer shifts, and no obligation to accept shifts.

And I suppose the question, though, the more difficult question, would there be any criteria around regularity? I think that would be quite hard to draft. Again, there would be a criteria of opportunity to get professional advice before accepting the agreement.

Perhaps some sort of recognition that- A statement of the reason as to why it was casual, perhaps like you see as required with fixed term employment agreements. There's an obligation to state a good bona fide reason why it's casual, could be a criteria.

Part of the way it's handled at the moment as well is we encourage our members to actually regularly review work patterns and contracts. So if someone is regularly on shift and that looks like it's going to continue, we do recommend that they move from a casual to a permanent part-time employment agreement. Thank you very much. I think that that's all we've got time for this morning.
Really appreciate the time you've taken to present to us, and for those new insights. Members, we're now moving back to the submission from Frank Owen Pirie. He's joining us via teleconference.

Frank, we've got five minutes with you this morning, including time for questions. Fire away. Hello? Yep, we can hear you. Hey, good morning. My name is Frank, and I recently graduated from university. I'm currently working as a chef while looking for work in design.

Although my contract says part-time, I work full-time hours. I've worked in hospitality since I was 16, and in all of those years, I've never seen a contract that actually reflects the hours people work. Most say 10 or 15 hours a week, but the reality is closer to 40. That's just how the industry operates.

So when this bill says leave should be based on contracted hours, not actual hours worked, that does not reflect reality. It ignores a huge part of the workforce and how the workforce actually works and lives. This isn't just my experience. Around 20% of workers are on part-time contracts, and about a quarter of work happens outside standard hours.

That means this bill affects a massive proportion of the workforce, especially people in hospitality, retail, and care. These are already lower paid roles, often filled by younger people, older workers, and caregivers, people who already have less security. Under this bill, someone can work full-time hours every week, but only build leave as if they're part-time.

So the more flexible and available you are, the less protected you become. That is backwards. In industries like hospitality and healthcare, where extra hours are normal, this will mean less sick leave and fewer protections for people who are already stretched. And when people don't have enough sick leave, they still come to work.

That's the reality. In my job, if I'm sick, I do not have leave. There is pressure to show up anyway, especially when places are short-staffed. But in hospitality and healthcare, that doesn't just affect the worker, it affects everyone. We've already seen what happens when illness spreads through workplaces. This bill increases that risk.

Paid leave isn't just a benefit, it is a health and safety protection. This bill shifts it into something closer to a trade-off. It creates a two-tier system, where people with stable hours keep their leave, but people with terrible hours, often the lowest paid, get actual less protection. That's not balance, that is inequity.

There is also real risk this encourages employers to keep contracts artificially low and rely on extra hours because it reduces their obligation to provide leave. So instead of protecting workers, it creates incentives to structure work in a way that gives them less. I also want to point out something concerning an interview on Radio NZ.

Minister Brooke van Velden has said she originally aimed for a version of the bill where no worker would be worse off, but that approach was abandoned after being deemed too complex for payroll systems to handle. So we are left with a bill where a large portion of workers will be worse off, and those workers are already among the most vulnerable.

And for what? Just to make payroll simpler? Even though we are living in 2026 with modern payroll systems widely available, that is not a strong enough reason to reduce people's rights. Simplifying admin shouldn't come at the cost of fairness. At its core, this bill shifts risk from employers to workers, especially those with the least power to absorb it.

To fix this, I recommend leave should reflect actual hours worked, not just contracted hours. All hours should accrue leave proportionately rather than being replaced with compensation. There should be a minimum floor leave entitlement for all workers, and there must be safeguards to stop standard hours from being set artificially low to reduce entitlement.

A leave system should provide certainty, fairness, and protection. Right now, this bill risks doing the opposite. It creates a system where the people working the hardest, most irregular hours, receive the least protection. A system that lets people work full-time but be treated like part-time isn't flexibility, it is exploitation with better paperwork.

Thank you. Thank you very much. Are there any questions from members? No questions. Thanks very much, Frank. I appreciate the time you've taken to present to us. Thank you. And we are now going to invite our final submitter, because I think the one individual has not been able to come back to us.
So Oceana Gold, are they ready for us, Clark?

Hello. Good morning. Good morning. Thanks for joining us. We've got 10 minutes with you this morning, including time for questions, so over to you. Thank you. I'm Alison Paul, and I am the Senior Vice President for Legal and Public Affairs for OceanaGold. And I'm Diana Halloran. I'm the People and Culture Manager for OceanaGold New Zealand.

So very quickly, a bit about OceanaGold. We are New Zealand's largest gold miner, and we operate two gold mines at the moment, one at Waihi and one down north of Dunedin at Macraes. So we employ about 1,200 employees across those two operations. Our wages are about 180 million every year.

About half of our workforce is unionized, mainly in the operating areas. And we run, at the moment, 210 different rosters. 210. So that's on an operation that runs for 365 days of the year, 24 hours a day, regardless of whether it's a public holiday and anything else. We just run continuously at both sites.

Those rosters cover an absolute multitude of patterns. So in some instances, we have people working on a Thursday through to Wednesday roster, for example. Night shift, day shift, all the permutations you could think of. And so what that results in is a large amount of complexity, and when people talk about ambiguity between the different pay calculation methods, so at the moment, you're talking about ordinary weekly pay, average daily pay, average weekly earnings, relevant daily pay, and you're applying that across hundreds of roster patterns and

different types of holiday, you just get this incredible complexity. So the term ambiguity in any given instance sounds as though it's a nice binary choice and you can err on the side of caution, make sure you are paying people at least enough, err on the side of caution and move on.

But the reality is, if we give an example of the recent Anzac Day holiday, that may have involved, in some instances, employees who had worked overtime. They were doing a Thursday to Wednesday roster, so they incorporated both the actual holiday on the Saturday and the Mondayized holiday on the Monday.

That was something, we use SAP, that was something that SAP, across about 800 employees, was unable to calculate. So suddenly, you are introducing manual processes regularly, highly manual processes for applying these different permutations of holiday.

So, when you apply that complexity across 210 different rosters, if you were to ask me, how do you err on the side of caution? We have to err on the side of caution by actually being incredibly manual in our checks and balances. It is very complicated, unnecessarily complicated, and our number one submission really is that these changes in the bill that we're looking at today can't come soon enough. We need to find a way to make this far, far simpler.

So the essence of our submission is around the two-year lead-in period. We think that we understand some of the reasoning behind it. We understand that there will be very big businesses like ourselves that need to reconfigure their systems. For some businesses or government departments, it may be a choice to take a couple of years to do that. For us, we will be embracing the change just as quickly as we can to make our life and the life of our employees simpler and make it a whole lot easier for any given employee to know what they're going to be paid during a period of leave.

There will be small employers for whom simply going to a new system needs some time. They will be running less complicated lives than we do in roster patterns. So I can see scope for two years lead in for some employers. It's not going to work for us, and we would like to be able to move to this simpler way of calculating holiday pay and the other forms of pay far, far sooner than two years'

time. So, that is the essence of our submission. And the rest of the submission follows along the same lines. There is no ability to contract out or conduct remediation processes or do any of the other things that could allow you to have a simpler life within less than this two-year lead-in period, and we would like to see that changed.

Happy to take questions. Thanks very much. Grant McCullagh. Yeah. So I'm just intrigued by your comments. So what you're effectively saying, under the current law, that there isn't a payroll system out there that can actually cope with the current complexities. Is that what you're saying to me? Absolutely, 100%.

And you anticipate that will be different with the new law? Yes, we do. The new law, quite frankly, is far simpler, and it looks at what someone has contracted to be paid on a day off. Okay. Thank you. Thank you. Appreciate that. Thank you.

Diana Turiona. Yeah, thank you for the submission. One of the things that we've heard from a number of submitters is around the issues around software. So, I'm interested, just following up what Grant was saying. So you're saying you've got some software that'll click in pretty quickly to sort all this stuff out?

Well, we use SAP Concur and SAP. So it's one of the big well-known, well-regarded international payroll systems. Dee, you might be able to talk about whether there's any other systems that are more localized, but to our knowledge, there is no system that can cope with the degree of complexity that is introduced by five different basic calculation approaches to all of the different leave types across 210 different rosters.

Yeah. Just simplification of the bill in its entirety would make it easier for the software providers to be able to develop a product. And that might be the timing that needs the lead in for the change. But I would anticipate that any simplification of the bill would be able to add to the employee's experience, the user experience, as well as organizational experience.

And we're not saying that we want something off the peg. So we configure our payroll system in conjunction with SAP and in conjunction with accounting and legal advice and other things so that we are making sure we're making the right payments. So you don't just plug and play, but the configuring of the system tells us that we have to manualize large parts of our process every time you get into, for this example I gave you around Anzac Day, something that the system can't be configured in advance to handle.

Got it. Any other questions? Thank you, members. Thank you for the submission, Alison. We really appreciate it, and I'm sure we'll see you again. And members, that concludes our subcommittee hearings. We'll join for the main committee a little bit later this morning
